title: Retargeted serine integrases for one-step, precise integration of large DNA sequences in human cells - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractSerine integrases can precisely integrate large DNA constructs into desired chromosomal sites but only if their natural target site is first installed into the recipient genome. Here, to retarget serine integrases to a desired genomic site, we develop a modular integrase (MINT) system for genome editing. Through a combination of structural modeling, single-round directed evolution and screening in human cells, we retargeted the specificity of the serine integrase Bxb1. We demonstrate the therapeutic potential of the MINT system by retargeting Bxb1 to the human AAVS1 and TRAC loci, where wild-type Bxb1 has no detectable activity. By combining MINT constructs with both known activity-increasing Bxb1 mutants and zinc-finger DNA-binding domains, we achieve efficiencies of 29% at the AAVS1 locus and 35% at the TRAC locus in K562 cells. To further demonstrate clinical potential, we achieved 29% GFP integration efficiencies at the TRAC locus in human T cells.

Fig. 1: Bxb1–DNA interaction mapping and DNA-binding domain engineering using directed evolution.Fig. 2: Performance of engineered Bxb1 variants at pseudosites in the human genome.Fig. 3: Retargeting Bxb1 to the human TRAC and AAVS1 loci.Fig. 4: Optimization of TRAC constructs.Fig. 5: Testing MINT TRAC constructs in primary human T cells.

Data availability

Amino acid sequences and DNA sequences of constructs used in this study are provided as Supplementary Information. Illumina sequencing data underlying all experiments were deposited to the National Center for Biotechnology Information Sequence Read Archive under BioProject PRJNA1450726 and have been publicly released. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

All custom scripts are available from GitHub (https://github.com/sangamo-therapeutics/mint-sgmo-scripts).

Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Performance of zinc fingers fused to small serine recombinase domains and performance of different wild-type Bxb1 constructs in K562 landing pad cell lines.a. Performance of Zinc Finger (ZF)-targeted small serine recombinase Gin and Tn3 hyperactive domains. The data shown in this graph is derived from separate experiments. In contrast to Bxb1, both Gin and Tn3 hyperactive domain fusion proteins can result in high levels of indels causing low product purity and hindering further improvement of target integration frequencies. We also observed indels within the assayed TI junction site. Data is derived from a PCR-based NGS. Data are presented as the mean from four biological replicates for ZF-Gin, and individual data points are shown for ZF-Tn3 fusions. b. Performance of wild-type Bxb1 against natural attB and attP target sequences in human cells. We first established K562 landing pad cell lines by installing the natural attB or attP sequence in the human AAVS1 locus. We noticed improved performance of Bxb1 with a C-terminal NLS compared to the construct with a N-terminal NLS. This guided future Bxb1 designs where all evolved variants presented in this study have a C-terminal NLS. We also noticed higher TI into the attB landing pad. Notably, only minimal levels of indels were observed within the landing pad target sequences. Data is derived from a PCR-based NGS. Data are presented as the mean ± s.d. from four biological replicates (eight for untreated sample).Source dataExtended Data Fig. 2 Bxb1 engineering.a. Integrase variant library screening configuration with pBxb1 libraries where the helix, loop, or hairpin submotif has been randomized and are transformed along with pTarget plasmids where corresponding positions of the attB and attP target sites are altered. Primers with NNK codon usage were used for randomization of each residue indicated with an X to generate the Bxb1 variant libraries. For the hairpin library, SSK was used instead at residue 322 to bias the randomization to an A or P at this position. b. Example sequence logo plots summarizing enriched peptide motifs at amino acid residue positions 231-237 for helix selections with the corresponding helix DNA targets for each selection shown above each sequence logo. The sequence logos represent 100 selected peptide sequences that best match the enriched patterns identified in all selected peptides (typically between 20,000 and 100,000 selected peptides are sequenced for each selection). Position 235 was not randomized during selection and is shown in grey. Helices with an arginine (R) at position 237 were frequently observed in selections for a C at position -9 of the DNA target, helices and a lysine (K) at position 237 were often observed in selections for a A at position -9 of the DNA target, helices with an asparagine (N) at position 234 were often observed in selections for a T at position -10 of the DNA target, helices with an alanine (A) or glycine (G) at position 234 were often observed in selections for A at position -10 of the DNA target, and a tryptophan (W) at position 233 was often observed in selections with a C at position -11 of the DNA target. c. Example logo plots summarizing the results of selections at amino acid positions 154-159 for each loop DNA target selection shown above each sequence logo. d. Example logo plots summarizing the results of selections at amino acid positions 314-325 for each hairpin DNA target selection shown above each sequence logo. Residues that were not randomized are shown in gray. e. Bxb1 variant specificity assay configuration where a single integrase variant previously isolated in the directed evolution assay is tested for activity across DNA target libraries varied at the indicated positions with N. Each individual pTarget in the library contains the same modification at both half-sites of the attB and attP sites. f. Example DNA specificity plots of selected loop variants. Shown is the mean of four biological replicates. g. Example DNA specificity plots of selected hairpin variants. Shown is the mean of a total of eight replicates; four biological replicates and two technical replicates thereof. The relevant region of the DNA target site used to select each hairpin variant is shown below each specificity plot.Extended Data Fig. 3 Identification of Bxb1 pseudo-sites in the human genome using an unbiased genome-wide specificity assay.a. List of 23 validated Bxb1 pseudo-sites in the human genome in bed format that were either identified through a computational search or experimentally (or both) using the assay shown in Supplementary Fig. 3 with all 16 dinucleotide donors. The central dinucleotides in each site are underlined. Only the portion corresponding to an attB site are shown in the table. Site-specific donors were used to validate TI using a PCR-based NGS assay. CD34 and Chr1 pseudo-sites were identified in an independent computational search designed to identify targets for Bxb1 variants; control experiments indicated that these sites were better targeted with wild-type Bxb1. b. Sequence logo for all 118 potential pseudo-sites with at least two independent integrations identified using the experimental assay shown in Supplementary Fig. 3. Positions -19 to -12 of the 236 half-sites of these 118 potential pseudo-sites are a mixture of four different distinct DNA motifs and sequence logos for these four different motifs are shown below the sequence logo of the full sites. c. Examples of half-sites from validated human pseudo-sites for wild-type Bxb1 that correspond to each of the four DNA sequence motifs shown in panel b. Positions in each half site that correspond to key bases in the relevant motif are underlined.Extended Data Fig. 4 Performance of Bxb1 variants against pseudo-sites in the human genome.a. The attB pseudo-site on chromosome 3 used for initial targeting and performance measurements of various Bxb1 helix domain variants against this target site; the target DNA sequence is shown as the reverse complement of the sequence in Extended Data Fig. 3a to make it easier to visualize the region we are targeting in this experiment. The plot shows results of a PCR-based NGS assay for Bxb1 variants bearing the indicated Bxb1 helices tested at this target site. SATALKR is the wild-type helix peptide sequence. Selected helix sequences that match enriched motifs and contain an S or T at position 234 were active at the chromosome 3 target even in the absence of wild-type Bxb1 indicating that such helices can interact with both half-sites in the genomic target as well as the attP target in the donor. Similar sequences were enriched in selections with other target sites, so we concluded that these types of helix sequences represent helices with poor specificity, and we computationally removed similar sequences from subsequent selections. In contrast, selected Bxb1 variants with an N at position 234 such as AGGNLKR were only active at the chromosome 3 target when mixed with wild-type Bxb1. DNA targeting specificity characterization of this helix indicated a dramatic shift in specificity towards a T at position -10 (Fig. 1f) consistent with the GTC sequence at positions -11 to -9 of the half-site we targeted with this helix selection b. On-target performance of a Bxb1 variant with combined helix and hairpin variations, and data summary of a genome-wide specificity evaluation using the assay described in Supplementary Fig. 3 with all 16 dinucleotide donors. Sites are listed in bed format. Left panel. Here, we combined the most promising selected hairpin LARGRRKWARYR with both the AGGNLKR helix and the more active, but less specific WSSSLKR helix. Both combinations retained full activity at the chromosome 3 site when combined with wild-type Bxb1 but show a substantial decrease without wild-type Bxb1. Thus, WSSSLKR in combination with LARGRRKWARYR now requires wild-type Bxb1 to achieve full integration activity. This indicates improved specificity vs. the WSSSLKR helix variant alone which is able to interact with both half sites of the chromosome 3 target as well as the attP site in the donor in the absence of wild-type Bxb1. Right panel. Since both AGGNLKR and LARGRRKWARYR were able to target the intended chromosome 3 half-site more specifically than the wild-type Bxb1 helix and hairpin, we reasoned the combination of this helix and hairpin should be even more specific for the chromosome 3 site and characterized this combination of helix and hairpin using an unbiased genome-wide specificity assay. This fully engineered Bxb1 variant had fewer candidate integration sites within the human genome than wild-type Bxb1 and we observed that the intended site on chromosome 3 had higher levels of integrations than any other site in the human genome. Note that the indicated Bxb1 variant has to be mixed with wild-type Bxb1 (included to bind the attP site on the donor) in order to be active at the intended chr3 target site so the genome-wide specificity assay was performed with a mixture of the indicated Bxb1 variant and wild-type Bxb1. The experiment was performed with a pool of 16 donor constructs containing all possible central dinucleotides. Average integration counts were determined using the average deduplicated read numbers across 2 samples using primers that separately amplify the attL and attR plasmid-genome junctions. The corresponding control sample where GFP was substituted for Bxb1 resulted in 0 sites; the entire dataset is provided in Supplementary Table 8 with the corresponding GFP dataset in Supplementary Table 9. c. Activity-testing of a Bxb1 helix variant from panel a. in human HepG2 cells. d. Sequence of two additional Bxb1 pseudo-sites in the human genome. e. Bxb1 peptide sequences of evolved Bxb1 variants that showed improved performance against the half-sites of pseudo-sites shown in Fig. 2 and Extended Data Fig. 3a. f. Screening data using synthetic DNA targets tested in human K562 cells that was used to identify the constructs shown in panel d. Activity is determined by the number of DNA sequence reads corresponding to recombined versions of each synthetic target; activity is normalized to the activity of wild-type Bxb1 alone against the same synthetic target site. g. Results from a PCR-based NGS assay demonstrating improved performance of evolved Bxb1 variants against their chromosomal endogenous targets in K562 cells. The presence of a wild-type Bxb1 expression construct is necessary to bind the wild-type attP sequence on the donor plasmid. Grey bars show % of reads indicative of TI events; red bars show % of reads indicative of small insertions or deletions (Indels) at the target site. Data are presented as the mean ± s.d. from four biological replicates.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 5 Screening system for testing Bxb1 variants against a plasmid library of artificial target sites in human K562 cells.a. Schematic of a system for testing evolved Bxb1 variants against thousands of artificial targets in a plasmid target library as described in Fig. 2a. b. Sequence examples for the target library and the donor, including assay design information for PCR-based detection of recombination events. c. Sequence example for all plasmid target library members of the AAVS1 site highlighted in Fig. 3. The plasmid target library includes up to six distinct members for each endogenous target site. Four members are designed to support the identification of Bxb1 variants targeting the RD and ZD motifs of both endogenous half-sites. Two additional members are designed to confirm activity of stacked Bxb1 variants against the corresponding half-sites. Bxb1 variants are screened individually against the plasmid target library. See Supplementary Tables 14-17 for all AAVS1 and TRAC target sites that were screened with this method.Extended Data Fig. 6 ZF and activity-increasing TRAC Bxb1 construct optimizations.a. Four linkers with different lengths were tested in the context of the constructs highlighted in Fig. 4. Linker 2 was chosen for all subsequent experiments. The results are the mean ± s.d. from three biological replicates. b. Additional sequence and genome annotation information on the top 5 candidate off-target integration sites, and the on-target, identified in the genome-wide specificity assay for TRAC-L-ZF-7 + TRAC-R-ZF-5 + WT. OT1 and OT2 were used for all off-target confirmation studies. While the donor dinucleotide in the experiment corresponded to GT (or AC if integration occurs on the bottom strand) it is of note that OT3 contains a GC here as per the hg38 reference genome and it is likely that the K562 cells used contained a mutation at this position.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 7 Optimization of Bxb1-ZFP variants targeting other sites.a. AAVS1, b. ABCA4, and c. RS1. Data are presented as the mean ± s.d. from four biological replicates for AAVS1 and RS1, and three biological replicates for ABCA4 respectively. ee, V74A + E229K + V375I. Detailed screening data for Bxb1-ZF variants and their corresponding recognition sites are provided in Supplementary Tables 21 and Supplementary Fig. 9. MINT reagents that target RS1 and ABCA4 were generated from a small archive of pre-characterized Bxb1 helix and hairpin variants (Supplementary Figs. 19,20 and Supplementary Tables 38-40) and did not require any custom bacterial selections. More details about the process used to generate the MINT reagents that target RS1 and ABCA4 can be found in Supplementary Tables 18-20. The RS1 and ABCA4 target sites were identified computationally from a list of disease-related genes, but we note that both sites are also on a list of 410,776 cryptic attB sequences (Bxb1 pseudo-sites) reported by a group from Tome Biosciences69. These 410,776 cryptic attB sequences occur approximately once every 7 kb in the human genome so we propose that others could target desired regions of the human genome without performing any bacterial selections by i) identifying cryptic attB sites within desired regions of the human genome, ii) screening the archive of Bxb1 variants described in Supplementary Figs. 19,20 against half-sites of desired cryptic attB sequences using the mammalian plasmid screening system we describe in this work, iii) combining the most promising helix and hairpin variants for a given half-site into a single composite Bxb1 variant, and iv) co-delivering a composite Bxb1 variant that targets the left half-site of a desired cryptic attB site, a composite Bxb1 variant that targets the right half-site of the same desired cryptic attB site, an appropriate donor construct, and a non-reprogrammed integrase if needed to bind the donor.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 8 Targeted integration characterization in GFP positive single-cell clones.a. The PCR results have been independently validated in at least two separate experiments. c. Flow cytometry of GFP expression in a biallelic clone. d.
